text: The surgeon uses a sponge to retract the small bowel away from the medial aspect of the sigmoid mesentery, preparing to begin the medial to lateral dissection.

I left this case largely unedited for the transvaginal extraction because that's the part that I really wanted to focus on with this video. So a lot of lateral dissection was left out. The anastomosis was left out just because I think you've seen that all before. And again, the purpose of this video is to highlight the ease of doing a transvaginal extraction for a cancer.

The surgeon isolates the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) for high ligation in a patient with a high BMI, using scissors to meticulously clear the vascular adventitia and expose the vessel wall. The vessel sealer is then used for the ligation. The upper rectum is mobilized, and the hypogastric plexus nerves are highlighted near the bedside.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection, joining the previously dissected medial plane, with visible nerves in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses scissors to perform lateral dissection, progressing towards the splenic flexure. A sponge is placed on top of the ureter for orientation. As the dissection approaches the left kidney, the surgeon switches to a vessel sealer for grasping and hemostasis. The redundant sigmoid and descending colon allow for sufficient mobilization without fully mobilizing the splenic flexure.

The surgeon encounters congenital adhesions of the omentum to the left anterior abdominal wall and carefully dissects them down. The dissection continues over the left kidney, revealing a small splenial at the top of the kidney. The vessel sealer is used for hemostasis during this process.
The surgeon mobilizes the left side of the rectum, having previously completed the dissection on the right. The distal mesentery is also being addressed during this step.

The surgeon inserts a twelve millimeter trocar as arm number three through the right rectus muscle, rather than the right lower quadrant, to achieve a better angle for stapling deeper in the pelvis. The trocar is positioned to allow closure of the anterior fascia without a full thickness suture passer.
The surgeon uses a stapler to divide the proximal section of the colon, observing the pumping blood flow, and then proceeds to divide the distal section, achieving a favorable angle for stapling.
The surgeon uses a 60mm stapler and elevates the uterus. An EEA sizer is placed into the posterior vagina, ensuring it is not too close to the anterior wall of the rectum.

The surgeon opens the posterior fornix of the vagina high in the pouch of Douglas and prepares a 15mm Endocatch bag for specimen extraction. The specimen is placed into the bag, which is then pulled out, allowing the specimen to slide out smoothly by opening the end of the bag outside the patient and using a ring clamp on the staple line.

The surgeon addresses bleeding from the corner of the vagina by closing the muscular wall with a 3-0 V-Loc suture. The vaginotomy appears larger than the initial incision due to tearing from the extraction of a larger specimen.

The surgeon uses the camera in arm number two positioned in the epigastric midline while suturing with arms number one and three. The muscular wall of the vagina is closed, and the surgeon proceeds to close the peritoneum. The left ureter is visible beyond the surgeon's left hand at the ten to eleven o'clock position.

The surgeon lines up the sewn anastomosis, having sewn the two posterior rows of the staple ends together with a 2-0 PDS suture. The inner layer is being sewn with a 3-0 V-Loc suture, one going to the left and one to the right. A rigid scope is used to ensure no stitching or tearing occurred during the transvaginal extraction.

The surgeon completes the second outer layer of suturing using IIOPDS, imbricating the inner IIIOV lock with precise bites of the bowel wall. The bowel is shown to come together without tension. The ureter is visible as the camera is repositioned to arm number three for the anastomosis. A leak test is performed using a nineteen millimeter proctoscope, confirming the anastomosis is hemostatic, widely patent, and without leaks.

The surgeon cleans the surgical field, showing the proximity of the rectal anastomosis and the vaginal suture line. The omentum is sutured to the pouch of Douglas, with the surgical field in a Trendelenburg position.

The surgeon holds the uterus up while using arm number one to position the omentum between the rectal and vaginal suture lines. A second stitch is placed to secure the omentum, ensuring it remains interposed. The uterus is then dropped down, and bipolar cautery is applied to the suture line to ensure hemostasis.
